name: Carnival of Binche
description: The Binche carnival, with roots dating back to the Middle Ages, is one of Europe's oldest surviving street carnivals. The legendary Gille characters, wearing ostrich-feather hats, wooden clogs, bells and wax masks, parade through the town on Mardi Gras. Pierrots, harlequins and peasants follow in procession, culminating with the Gilles' dancing in the Grand Place under fireworks.
timing: Three days preceding Lent (Shrove Sunday through Mardi Gras)

name: Carnival of Barranquilla
description: Every year during the four days before Lent, the Carnival de Barranquilla offers a repertory of dances and musical expressions originating from different Colombian sub-cultures. European, African, and indigenous peoples and cultures have converged to create this celebration on Colombia's Caribbean coast. Features include dances (mico y micas, African congo, paloteo), musical genres (cumbia, puya, porro), handcrafted objects including floats, costumes, head ornaments and animal masks.

name: Carnival of Oruro
description: The Carnival of Oruro, situated at 3,700 metres altitude in western Bolivia on a former pre-Columbian ceremonial site, lasts ten days and gives rise to popular arts in masks, textiles and embroidery. The main event is the entrada procession: more than 28,000 dancers and 10,000 musicians in about 50 groups walk 4 kilometres, repeating the journey for 20 hours without interruption. The traditional llama llama (diablada) in worship of the Uru god Tiw became the main dance, blending Andean religion with Christian liturgy.

name: Busó festivities at Mohács: masked end-of-winter carnival custom
description: A six-day carnival in late February to mark the end of winter. Named for the busós, frightening-looking costumed people wearing wooden masks and big woolly cloaks. Over 500 busós arrive in rowboats on the Danube for a march through the city. A coffin symbolizing winter is burned on a bonfire in the central square. The tradition originated with the Croatian minority in Mohács.

name: Ijele masquerade
description: In communities in Anambra state in south-eastern Nigeria, celebrations and burial ceremonies feature the performance of the Ijele masquerade. The mask is about four metres tall — so large that it takes a hundred men six months to prepare. Divided into upper and lower segments by a large python at the centre, the ijele is constructed of colourful fabric on bamboo sticks and decorated with figurines. The towering masked figure dances at the culmination of other masquerades, protected by six 'police' and carrying a mirror. Mask carriers are chosen by ballot and seclude themselves for three months beforehand.
timing: Dry season — celebrations, burial ceremonies and special occasions

name: Carnaval de Negros y Blancos (Black and White Carnival)
description: Arising from native Andean and Hispanic traditions, this great celebration lasts from 28 December to 6 January in San Juan de Pasto in south-western Colombia. Begins on 28 December with the Carnival of Water; includes the Old Year's parade with satirical figures. On the main days people of all ethnicities don black cosmetics then white talcum to symbolize equality. It is especially important as an expression of mutual desire for tolerance and respect.

name: Annual carnival bell ringers' pageant from the Kastav area
cities: Kastav area (Halubje, Bregi, Brgud, Mučići, Mune, Rukavac, Zvoneća, Žejane, Frlanija)
description: During the January carnival period, bell ringers march through villages in the Kastav region in north-west Croatia. Clothed in sheepskin throws with bells around their waists and sporting distinctive hats with evergreen sprigs, groups of two to thirty ringers swagger behind a guide carrying a small evergreen tree. They bump each other's hips rhythmically and leap into the air. When reaching a village, bell ringers form concentric circles in the town square, ringing fiercely until residents offer food and a rest. At carnival's end, the ringers collect rubbish from each house and burn it in front.
timing: January carnival period (17 January to Ash Wednesday)

name: Shrovetide door-to-door processions and masks in the villages of the Hlinecko area
description: The Shrovetide processions take place in the town of Hlinsko and six nearby villages in Eastern Bohemia. Village men and boys, disguised in masks depicting traditional characters (red masks for boys and black for married men), go from door to door accompanied by a brass band. Four of the men perform a ritual dance at each house to secure a rich harvest and prosperity. A symbolic 'Killing of the Mare' ritual takes place after the last house has been visited, followed by a dance. The processions were banned by the Catholic Church and later by the socialist government.

name: Aalst carnival
description: The three-day Aalst Carnaval begins on the Sunday before Lent. Features a Prince Carnaval who symbolically becomes mayor; a procession of effigies of giants and 'Bayard'; a broom dance in the central market; a parade of young men dressed as women; and a ritual burning of the carnival effigy. Draws up to 100,000 spectators. The 600-year-old ritual is a collective effort of all social classes.
timing: Three days beginning Sunday before Lent
removal_note: Removed from the Representative List in 2019 (Decision 14.COM 12) due to antisemitic floats

name: Winter festivities, Carnival of Podence
description: A social practice initially functioning as a rite of passage for men, now extended to women and children. The Caretos — masked characters wearing tinplate or leather masks, costumes covered with colourful wool fringes and small bells — dance around women with cowbells during the three-day street festival. On Monday night, a group of men announce a fictional list of engaged couples, satirizing them and eliciting collective laughter. On Shrove Tuesday, some people mask themselves as 'matrafonas'. The ritual of burning a Shrovetide figure takes place Tuesday afternoon.
timing: Three days: Shrovetide (Shrove Sunday to Shrove Tuesday)

name: Basel Carnival
description: Beginning on the Monday following Ash Wednesday and lasting exactly 72 hours, Basel Carnival is the largest carnival in Switzerland. Two parades on Monday and Wednesday bring together 11,000 costumed carnivalists in parades of fife and drum cliques, floats and carriages. Tuesday is dedicated to children with concerts and lantern exhibitions. Around 20,000 people actively participate; approximately 200,000 visitors attend. The carnival contributes to social cohesion and promotes tolerance through social criticism.
timing: 72 hours from Monday after Ash Wednesday

name: Carnival of Granville
description: A four-day celebration preceding Shrove Tuesday. Opens with the mayor handing the keys to King Carnival (a papier mache figure), then a series of float processions interspersed with marching bands. About 40 floats take a humorous look at current events, politics and celebrities — involving 2,500 'carnivalists' who spend six months creating them. Social balls and a confetti battle in the town square are held. The festivities finish with a 'night of intrigues' when carnival-goers disguised in costume joke with loved ones. Finally, the king is sentenced and cremated in the port. Attracts 100,000 spectators annually.

name: Carnival of El Callao, a festive representation of a memory and cultural identity
description: Associated with cannes brulées emancipation celebrations in French-speaking Caribbean islands. Running from January to March, features parades of people dressed as characters from history and fantasy, as well as calypso music. The parades are led by the madamas (Antillean matrons communicating values, dancing in colourful dresses); the medio-pintos (young people smudging charcoal on people who don't give a donation); the mineros (gold miners); and the diablos (masked people carrying a whip to maintain order). Up to 3,000 participants.

name: Bear festivities in the Pyrenees
description: Winter festivities taking place in five Pyrenean villages in France and Andorra. Young men dress up as bears and run through the streets trying to catch participants. The festivities are punctuated by dances, speeches in Catalan and music. The scenario symbolizes the end of winter, spring rebirth, and the relationship between humans and nature. After declining in the 1960s, the festivities have regained popularity. Unites thousands of people from the Catalan region.

name: Processional giants and dragons in Belgium and France
description: Traditional processions of huge effigies of giants, animals or dragons. These effigies first appeared in urban religious processions in the fourteenth century. The giants and dragons are large-scale models up to nine metres in height and up to 350 kilos. They represent mythical heroes or animals, historical and legendary characters or trades. Giants and dragons enliven popular festivals at least once a year, each with its specific feast day. Note: The Ducasse of Ath was removed from this element in 2022.
timing: Various dates (each effigy has a specific feast day)
removal_note: Ducasse of Ath removed from element in 2022

name: Shakespeare Mas', a traditional component of Carriacou's annual carnival
description: Shakespeare Mas' combines speechmaking, costume design and public theatre. Practitioners called 'kings' compete against each other, reciting passages from Shakespeare's Julius Caesar, historical texts, Bible verses and rhetorical boasts. Incorrect recitations are met with a symbolic lash on a padded headpiece. An integral part of Carriacou's annual Carnival celebrations, it may reflect an imitation of the colonial classroom. A 100-year-old practice that rose from the diverse cultures brought together under slavery. Skills are transmitted through school curricula and organized activities.

name: Patum of Berga
description: A popular festival in this Catalan town north of Barcelona with origins in medieval festivities accompanying Corpus Christi. Theatrical performances and parades of effigies animate the streets. Characters include the Tabal (large drum), Cavallets (papier mâché horses), Maces (fire demons), Guites (mule dragons), an eagle, giant-headed dwarves, Plens (fire demons), and giants dressed as Saracens. All characters join to perform the final dance, the Tirabol. The Patum has preserved its mix of profane and religious features through centuries.
timing: Week of Corpus Christi (late May to late June)
